About FAQ

Heparin Management for CPB Perfusion

Estimate UFH bolus guidance with TBW/IBW/ABW strategy, ACT checkpoints, and obesity safety cues for perfusion workflows.

Heparin dosing in CPB is weight-indexed but should be interpreted with ACT trend, hemodilution, temperature, and local protocol.

This page focuses on adult perfusion use: initial UFH bolus planning, additional bolus guidance, and resistance/reversal context as quick bedside support.

Heparin Management (CPB)

Calculate an initial loading dose with weight strategy safeguards and optional flow targets; toggle resistance cues when ACT response is low.

Patient parameters

Adult CPB

Height should be 50–250 cm

Weight should be 20–300 kg

ACT monitoring per protocol

Auto switches to ABW when BMI ≥ 30 to reduce overdose risk.

Auto logic: BMI < 30 → TBW; BMI 30–39 → ABW (0.4 correction); BMI ≥ 40 → ABW (0.3) with obesity safety cues.

Devine IBW: Male = 50 + 0.91 × (cm − 152.4), Female = 45.5 + 0.91 × (cm − 152.4).

ABW 0.4 / 0.3 rules: ABW = IBW + factor × (TBW − IBW); factor 0.4 when BMI 30–39, factor 0.3 when BMI ≥ 40.

Safety cap: Obesity flows/doses use IBW/ABW and may cap BSA to avoid extreme UFH and hemodilution.

Initial dose (u/kg)

Risk factors (check all)

Selecting risk factors does not automatically change dosing. (Reference only)

Next steps (reference)

  • Rule out basics: confirm heparin delivery, sampling/ACT device issues, temperature/hemodilution.
  • Recheck ACT at 3–5 min; if below target, consider additional UFH per local protocol.
  • If inadequate response persists, consider checking AT activity and/or anti-Xa (or heparin concentration) and prepare AT supplementation (FFP if AT concentrate not available) per protocol.
  • Special situations (e.g., HIT) → follow institutional DTI protocol.

Enter patient parameters to calculate

Height, weight, and dose are required.

BMI

-

IBW (Devine)

- kg

Devine formula

BSA (Mosteller)

- m²

Capped for Safety (Obesity)

Dosing weight

- kg

Pediatric safety notice

This calculator is validated for adult CPB only. For patients < 20 kg or height < 120 cm, follow institutional pediatric protocols.

Extreme obesity safety notice

BMI ≥ 50 kg/m². Dosing weight uses ABW (0.3) to avoid extreme UFH doses. Consider Anti-Xa or heparin concentration monitoring rather than ACT alone.

Initial bolus

-

High dose > 40k U
Additional bolus-

Sensitivity (weight strategy vs dose @300 u/kg)

StrategyWeightDose / Δ
TBW--
ABW (0.4)--
IBW--

In severe obesity, TBW-based dosing can exceed 40,000 U; reassess dosing when values climb this high.

Quick protocol

  1. Bolus -
  2. Wait 3–5 min → Check ACT
  3. Monitor ACT q30min during CPB
  4. If ACT low: + bolus

Morbid obesity management

Using ABW with 0.3 correction to prevent overdose. Flow capped to reduce hemodilution; monitor SvO₂ and lactate closely.

Clinical pearls

  • ACT is influenced by hemodilution, hypothermia, platelet count, factor levels, antithrombin activity, and device-specific variability.
  • TBW-based heparin dosing is commonly used for CPB initiation, but it may produce very large doses in severe obesity.
  • IBW-based dosing may reduce overdose risk in obesity, but IBW alone can underdose if ACT, heparin concentration, or anti-Xa is not confirmed.
  • ABW is a compromise between TBW and IBW and may be useful when TBW appears excessive but IBW seems too conservative.
  • Failure to reach target ACT after an adequate bolus should prompt reassessment for heparin resistance, low antithrombin, sampling error, circuit factors, or drug/device effects.

TBW — Total Body Weight

TBW is actual measured body weight and is commonly used for initial CPB heparin dosing. In severe obesity, TBW can generate very large bolus estimates that may increase bleeding concern without reliably improving anticoagulation.

IBW — Ideal Body Weight

IBW is height/sex-based and may be useful when adiposity is high and heparin distribution is non-linear. IBW can reduce overdose risk, but under-anticoagulation remains possible unless ACT, heparin concentration, or anti-Xa confirms response.

ABW — Adjusted Body Weight

ABW provides an intermediate dosing weight between IBW and TBW when TBW appears excessive and IBW too conservative.

ABW = IBW + 0.4 × (TBW − IBW)

Use as a comparison reference, not a universal replacement for local protocol.

How to apply TBW, IBW, and ABW in practice

  • Non-obese adult CPB patients: TBW-based dosing is usually the simplest starting point.
  • Mild to moderate obesity: TBW may still be used, but compare IBW or ABW if calculated dose appears unusually high.
  • Severe obesity (especially BMI ≥ 35 kg/m²): display TBW, IBW, and ABW side by side; TBW may overestimate and IBW may underestimate need.
  • Known heparin resistance or low antithrombin: do not rely on weight dosing alone; confirm with ACT response and consider anti-Xa/heparin concentration per protocol.
  • High bleeding-risk cases: lower calculated dose is not automatically safer if anticoagulation is inadequate.

Obesity-specific interpretation

In obesity, blood volume and lean mass do not rise in direct proportion to total body weight, and adipose tissue has limited heparin distribution. Treat this heparin dose calculator as a comparison aid for CPB heparin dosing and cardiopulmonary bypass anticoagulation planning.

  • TBW dose: conventional actual-weight estimate.
  • IBW dose: conservative height-based estimate.
  • ABW dose: intermediate estimate that partially accounts for excess body weight.
  • ACT response: determines whether the selected dose achieved the anticoagulation target.
  • Anti-Xa or heparin concentration: useful when ACT is unreliable or discordant.

Safest interpretation: calculated dose + measured anticoagulation response + patient context.

Heparin resistance and ACT interpretation

Suspect heparin resistance when expected UFH bolus does not achieve institutional ACT target. Contributors include low antithrombin, elevated heparin-binding proteins, inflammation, thrombocytosis, prior heparin exposure, and high factor VIII/fibrinogen.

ACT is rapid point-of-care guidance but not a pure heparin concentration test; values are affected by hypothermia, hemodilution, platelet count, factor levels, device type, aprotinin exposure, and sampling conditions.

  • Confirm sample quality and device validity.
  • Confirm full bolus delivery.
  • Consider additional heparin per institutional protocol.
  • Consider anti-Xa or heparin concentration testing when available.
  • Consider targeted antithrombin supplementation when AT-mediated resistance is likely.

Guideline anchors (summary)

  • STS/SCA/AmSECT 2018: Initial heparin dosing and monitoring should confirm adequate anticoagulation before CPB, commonly with ACT-based institutional targets.
  • ACT target: many protocols use thresholds around ≥ 400–480 seconds, but exact targets depend on local protocol, device type, and surgical context.
  • Heparin concentration / anti-Xa: consider additional monitoring when ACT is unreliable or discordant (hypothermia, hemodilution, inflammation, obesity).
  • Antithrombin: UFH effect depends on antithrombin activity; low AT can contribute to heparin resistance and may require targeted management.
  • Obesity: TBW, IBW, and ABW are dosing references, not automatic orders; final dosing should be confirmed by measured anticoagulation response.

Selected references

  • Shore-Lesserson L, Baker RA, Ferraris VA, et al. STS/SCA/AmSECT Clinical Practice Guidelines: Anticoagulation during Cardiopulmonary Bypass. Ann Thorac Surg. 2018.
  • Wahba A, et al. 2024 EACTS/EACTAIC/EBCP Guidelines on cardiopulmonary bypass in adult cardiac surgery. Eur J Cardiothorac Surg. 2025.
  • Vienne M, et al. Adjusted calculation model of heparin management during cardiopulmonary bypass in obese patients. 2018.
  • Bull BS, Korpman RA, Huse WM, Briggs BD. Heparin therapy during extracorporeal circulation. J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg. 1975.
